Standard size 3x BB to 300 total, given stacks allow (my 78BB, opponents deeper). Builds pot with position and initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand to play out of position. Elon, the button, has raised to 300. Given that T3o has poor playability and I am sandwiched between an aggressive opener and a deep-stacked Big Blind, folding is the most prudent move to conserve my stack as we approach the end of the session.

Folding Tc4h (trash hand) against Elon's button raise. With only 4 hands remaining and a weak hand out of position, this is a clear fold. Even accounting for Elon's likely wide range due to short stack, my hand lacks the strength to justify calling. Preserving chips for premium hands is the correct strategy in the final hands.

On the Button in a three-handed game, Ace-Five offsuit (A5o) is a standard opening hand. My goal is to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds. Given it's Hand 148 of 150, maintaining stack stability is important, but folding such a hand on the button would be overly tight. I will raise to 300, which has been the consistent standard open size throughout the session.

Called Flash's 300 raise with 8d Qd from Small Blind. This is a reasonable hand to see a flop with given solid stack position, decent high card value, flush potential, and good pot odds. With the largest stack, I can control the hand post-flop and outplay weaker opponents. A raise would be overly aggressive here when calling gives more flexibility to respond to the BB's action and board texture.

KCs Tc is a strong suited broadway hand from the BB facing a button raise to 3BB and an SB call. Getting excellent pot odds (200 to call into 700 pot, ~3.5:1), and the hand has good playability post-flop with top-pair potential, straight potential, and flush draw capability. Multiway pot likely, but OOP is acceptable at these odds. Folding is too tight; raising risks bloating pot OOP with a non-premium hand against two players who may defend wide based on history. Calling is +EV and standard defense here with deep stacks.

Betting top pair (Queen) with an 8 kicker on a dry flop (Qs9c2s). This hand is strong and should extract value. The board has limited draw potential, and aggressive betting maintains control of the hand against opponents who showed pre-flop aggression. With the largest stack, I can apply pressure while extracting value from weaker holdings. Standard flop bet sizing around 50% of the 900-chip pot is appropriate here.

Flop of Qs9c2s completely misses my KcTc. I have overcards (K), a live Ten, and a weak backdoor club flush draw, but no made hand, pair, or strong draw. Facing a small blind lead of 450 into 900 (now pot 1350, 3:1 odds), but in a 3-way pot OOP with button raiser Flash still to act behind me. Claude's SB leading range likely includes Qx, 9x, Ax suited spade draws, sets, or bluffs, against which my equity is marginal (~25-30% at best, but less multiway). Calling commits ~6% of my stack OOP with poor playability if Flash raises or continues aggression. Raising is overplay with air. Pot odds not sufficient given reverse implied odds and dynamic range. Folding preserves stack for better spots in final hands; hand defended pre-flop but board texture favors folding now.

On a board of Qs-9c-2s, I have Ace-high (Ad5c). Claude (Small Blind) has led out for 450 into a 900 pot (a half-pot bet) after I raised pre-flop and Elon called. This 'donk lead' from the Small Blind into two players usually indicates either a marginal made hand (like a 9, a weak Q, or a pocket pair) or a draw (like spades or a straight draw). I have very little equity here with just overcards and no real backdoor potential other than an Ace. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack with only 3 hands remaining in the session.

Raising with Ad-5h on the button is optimal. Ace is a premium preflop hand, and I have excellent position. A 3x raise to 300 total builds the pot, establishes aggression, and leverages my positional advantage. With the largest stack, I can apply controlled pressure. This is standard +EV play with a strong hand in position.

K8o is a marginal hand out of position in the small blind facing a button raise to 3bb. Claude on the button has a wide raising range, but calling OOP with offsuit king-eight offers poor playability post-flop and requires hitting the king or strong draw. Pot odds are decent (calling 250 into 450+call), but implied odds are limited without suited connectors or better equity. Folding preserves my 7600 stack for the final hand #150, avoiding a likely tough spot OOP. Raising is not justified with this hand lacking domination potential or blockers. Opponents' stacks are deeper, but discipline maximizes long-term EV in this late stage.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Trey suited. Claude, on the button, has raised to 300. Elon has folded from the small blind. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. Ace-Trey suited is a strong hand to defend with heads-up, especially with the suitedness and the nut flush potential. Since we are at hand 149 of 150, I want to see a flop and potentially win a decent pot, but I see no need to over-inflate the pot pre-flop with a 3-bet against a likely wide button range. I will call to see the flop.

On this flop of 8c Qs 8h, I have Ace-high with a backdoor club flush draw. The board is paired, which reduces the likelihood that either of us has a strong hand unless someone has an Eight or a Queen. As the Big Blind defender against a button open, I will check to the aggressor. My hand has some showdown value and can certainly improve on many turns. Checking allows me to see how Claude reacts and preserves my stack for the final two hands of the game.

Betting 450 on the flop with Ad-5h against Flash's weakness. I raised preflop with a premium Ace and need to maintain aggression with my strong holding. Flash checked from the BB, indicating vulnerability. A continuation bet of 450 (roughly 2/3 pot) is standard sizing - strong enough to fold weaker hands and build the pot, but not overcommitting. My button position combined with the largest stack allows me to apply controlled pressure. This is +EV poker with a strong holding against demonstrated weakness.

On a paired board of 8-Q-8, Claude's continuation bet as the pre-flop button aggressor is very standard and doesn't necessarily indicate a strong hand. My Ace-high (Ac 3c) has reasonable showdown value in this heads-up situation and I hold a backdoor club flush draw. With pot odds of approximately 2.4:1 and only one hand remaining after this, I will call the flop bet to see if the turn improves my hand or if Claude slows down, allowing me to potentially win with Ace-high at showdown.

On the turn, an Ace appears, giving me top pair. However, my kicker (Trey) is very weak, and the board is paired with Eights. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop and flop aggressor, checking is the most prudent move. It allows me to control the size of the pot and see how Claude reacts to the Ace, which is a card that hits my calling range well. If Claude checks back, I likely have the best hand; if he bets, I have a relatively easy call to see the river, given the showdown value of top pair in a heads-up pot.

Betting 800 on the turn with top pair (Aces). The turn Ah is perfect for my hand - I have the best Ace. Flash has checked twice (flop and turn), demonstrating weakness, which suggests holdings like Qx or 8x that struggle against my pair. My preflop aggression combined with strong position on the button allows me to apply controlled pressure with a strong holding. This is a standard value bet (approximately 1/2 pot) that builds the pot while keeping Flash engaged with medium-strength hands. Stack sizes are healthy for continued play on the river.

On the turn, an Ace appears, giving me top pair. Claude continues to bet, now 800 into a pot of 1550. Given the pot odds (roughly 3:1) and the fact that we are heads-up, folding top pair is too conservative, despite my weak kicker. I will call to see the river and evaluate based on the final board and Claude's action.

On the river, the board is 8c Qs 8h Ah Td. I have top pair with Aces, but my kicker (3) is the weakest possible. The board is paired with Eights, and an Ace appeared on the turn, which I called. Claude has been the aggressor throughout the hand. Checking is the most strategic move here; it allows me to control the pot size, potentially see a cheap showdown, or evaluate Claude's action if he chooses to bet. Given the weak kicker, betting into Claude would likely only result in being called by hands that beat me (better Aces, Queens, or Eights).

River value bet with top pair (Aces with 5 kicker). Flash has checked after calling down on flop and turn, indicating weakness. This river check represents an opportunity to extract value. A bet of 800 (roughly 1/4 of the 3150 pot) is standard river value sizing—strong enough to build the pot but not so large that it folds out all of Flash's weaker holdings like Qx or low pairs. Flash's calling pattern suggests they have a marginal hand that will likely call a moderate river bet. This maximizes value with a premium holding against demonstrated weakness.

Claude has been the aggressor on every street, but the river bet sizing of 800 into a pot of 3,150 is relatively small (about 25% of the pot). This small sizing often indicates a thin value bet or a marginal hand trying to get to showdown cheaply. While my kicker (Trey) is the weakest possible for my top pair of Aces, the pot odds are excellent (nearly 6:1). In a heads-up situation on hand 149 of 150, folding top pair to such a small bet would be overly exploitable. Raising is not an option as it would likely only be called by better hands. I will call to see the showdown and protect my stack for the final hand.

3-handed on the button preflop with Ac9h. This is a strong opening hand: ace-high, good blockers, plays well postflop. Standard to raise first-in from button in short-handed play to steal blinds or build pot when called. Opponents deep but button position allows wide range. Raise to 300 (3x BB), a standard sizing based on game history and deep stacks. Amount is total bet of 300.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 3d, which is a very weak hand. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. This is the final hand of the game, and my goal is to maximize my stack. I am out of position against Elon and likely behind both his range and Claude's range in the Big Blind. Calling or raising with such a weak hand is mathematically incorrect and would likely lead to losing more chips in the long run. I will fold and preserve my current stack.

As5s is a marginal hand with weak kicker. While receiving 2.25:1 pot odds, my equity is right at the breakeven threshold (~31%). Out of position against Elon's short-stack range, this hand is difficult to play postflop. With a healthy stack and no obligation to gamble on weak hands in the final hand, folding is the prudent choice.
